expressions.dialogue:
Joey: I can't believe it's Christmas already. Ya know, I mean, one day your eatin' turkey, **the next thing ya know**, your lords are a-leapin' and you geese are a-layin'.
Chandler: Which is why geese are so relaxed this time of year.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 어느새, 순식간에, 그러다 보면 어느 순간
expressions.nuance: 시간이 빠르게 지나가거나 예상치 못한 일이 갑자기 벌어졌을 때 쓰는 표현. 약간 과장된 이야기나 유머러스한 맥락에서도 자주 나옴.
expressions.example: One day you're a college freshman, the next thing you know, you're at your graduation. → 어느 날 대학 신입생이었는데, 어느새 졸업식에 서 있어.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Yeah. I was just in there. He introduced himself and the next thing I know, we're making out. You know.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: So I hide in the shower and the next thing you know they're going at it right on the bathroom floor.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: And so I had a lot of work to do so Ross, nice guy that he is, offered to help me out. And then we had a little wine, we got to talking, and the next thing you know out of nowhere Ross comes on to me.

expressions.dialogue:
Gang: Joey!
Joey: Oh, but when Phoebe has a problem, **everyone's all ears**!
Gang: Joey!
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 귀 기울여 듣다, 완전히 집중해서 듣다
expressions.nuance: 'I'm all ears'는 '어서 말해봐, 다 들을게'라는 뜻. 상대방이 할 말이 있을 때 적극적으로 들을 준비가 됐음을 나타내며, 여기서 Joey는 불평하는 맥락으로 씀.
expressions.example: You said you had something important to tell me — I'm all ears. → 중요한 얘기 있다고 했잖아, 다 들을게.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: I can't hear a word you're saying, my ears are ringing so bad.

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: Hey Gunther, you got stairs in your place?
Gunther: Yeah.
Ross: Here, **go nuts**. [gives him the Slinky]
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.nuance: 무언가를 자유롭게 써도 된다고 허락하거나 마음껏 누리라고 할 때 쓰는 캐주얼한 표현. '신나게 해봐'라는 가벼운 격려의 의미도 있음.
expressions.example: There's a whole pizza left — go nuts. → 피자 한 판 남았어, 실컷 먹어.

occ.speaker: Dr. Burke
occ.text: OK. You better. Oh God, here we go. Hey wanna see 'em go nuts? Watch this. [grabbing some wine glasses and opening the door to the party] Who needs glasses? [everyone laughs]

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Well, you're making him a frieze salad with goat cheese and pine nuts, wild nuts, wild rice, roast asparagus, and salmon au croup.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: All right, here you go buddy. (He tosses him one.) Go nuts. (Exits.)

expressions.dialogue:
Chandler: What's in the bag?
Ross: Um, just some presents.
Joey: C'mon show us what you bought. **You know you want to.**
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.nuance: 상대가 망설이고 있지만 사실은 하고 싶어한다는 걸 꿰뚫어 보고 장난스럽게 유도하는 표현. 가벼운 압박이나 놀림의 뉘앙스가 있음.
expressions.example: Just try on the dress. You know you want to. → 그냥 그 드레스 입어봐. 입어보고 싶은 거 알잖아.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Oh! You know my friend Abby who shaves her head? She said that if you want to break the bad boyfriend cycle, you can do like a cleansing ritual.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Hey, you guys all know what you want to do.

occ.speaker: Rach
occ.text: All right, you know what, that's fine. If you guys want to be children about this, that's fine. I do not need to see it. [Rachel grabs the paper and runs across the room, reading it to herself.]

expressions.dialogue:
Rachel: Horrible and degrading list of reasons not to be with me?
Ross: How 'bout **from now on** we just call it the 'unfortunate incident'?
[Rachel walks off]
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.nuance: 과거 일을 뒤로하고 새로운 약속이나 방식을 제안할 때 씀. 여기서는 Ross가 민감한 상황을 피하려는 의도로 쓰고 있어 약간 구차한 뉘앙스도 담김.
expressions.example: From now on, I'm going to bed before midnight every night. → 앞으로는 매일 자정 전에 잠자리에 들 거야.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Okay, from now on, you don't get to talk to other people.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: I am telling you, years from now, schoolchildren will study it as one of the greatest first dates of all time. It was unbelievable! We could totally be ourselves, we didn't have to play any games...

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Y'know what, it doesn't matter, 'cause she picked me. Me! From now on I get the dates and you have to stay home on Saturday nights watching Ready, Set, Cook!

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: Oh, actually this year we just made him homemade cookies.
Chandler: **And twenty-five it is.**
Joey: You gave him cookies?
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.nuance: 상대방의 말에 영향을 받아 재빨리 자신의 결정을 바꾸거나 확정할 때 쓰는 표현. 약간 체념하거나 현실적으로 타협하는 느낌이 담겨 있음.
expressions.example: 'The venue only holds fifty people.' 'And fifty guests it is.' → '장소에 50명밖에 못 들어가요.' '그러면 50명으로 결정이야.'

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Alright, so I'm back in high school, I'm standing in the middle of the cafeteria, and I realize I am totally naked.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Oh, uh, that would be mine. See, I wrote a note to myself, and then I realised I didn't need it, so I balled it up and... (sees that Monica is glaring at him) ...now I wish I was dead.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: You know.. you let your guard down, you start to really care about someone, and I just- I- (starts chewing her hair)

expressions.expression: I smell smoke. Maybe that's 'cause someone's pants are on fire.
expressions.dialogue:
Grandmother: Is it really your fa— well of course it is.
Phoebe: OK, **I smell smoke. Maybe that's 'cause someone's pants are on fire.**
Grandmother: Look, I...
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.nuance: 영미권 동요 'Liar, liar, pants on fire'에서 유래. '거짓말하는 거 다 보여'를 재치 있게 에둘러 말하는 방식으로, 직접적이지 않지만 날카로운 뉘앙스가 있음.
expressions.example: You said you were home all evening, but I saw your car downtown. I smell smoke. → 저녁 내내 집에 있었다고 했는데, 시내에서 차 봤거든. 거짓말하는 냄새 나는데.

expressions.dialogue:
Grandmother: How's it going?
Phoebe: Well, not so good. Upstate's pretty big, he's pretty small, **you do the math**.
Grandmother: Well, I think you're better off without him.
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.nuance: 결론이 너무 명백해서 굳이 설명할 필요 없다고 느낄 때 씀. 약간 냉소적이거나 체념한 느낌이 섞이기도 함.
expressions.example: It's a three-hour drive and we have half a tank. You do the math. → 세 시간 거리인데 기름이 반밖에 없어. 알아서 계산해봐.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Hey, I got one keyhole and about a zillion keys. You do the math.

expressions.dialogue:
Rachel: Phoebe, I thought your dad was in prison.
Phoebe: No, that's my stepdad. My real dad's the one that **ran out on** us before I was born.
Rachel: How have you never been on Oprah?
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.nuance: 책임져야 할 상황에서 상대방을 내버려두고 떠난 경우에 씀. 배신감이나 분노가 섞인 강한 부정적 뉘앙스.
expressions.example: He ran out on his family when things got tough. → 상황이 어려워지자 그는 가족을 버리고 도망쳤어.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Yeah! Y'know, ever since I ran out on Barry at the wedding, I have wondered whether I made the right choice. And now I know.

occ.speaker: Mindy
occ.text: Well uh, after you ran out on your wedding, Barry's parents told people that you were sort of....insane.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Look Monica, getting cold feet is very common. Y'know, it's-it's just because of all the anticipation and you just have to remember that you love Chandler. And also, I ran out on a wedding. You don't get to keep the gifts.

expressions.dialogue:
Phoebe: No I just, just wanted to know who he was, ya know.
Grandmother: I think you're better off without him. To me he's still the irresponsible creep who **knocked up** your mom and stole her Gremlin.
Phoebe: No I just, just wanted to know who he was.
expressions.speaker: Grandmother
expressions.nuance: 계획되지 않은 임신, 특히 무책임한 상황을 묘사할 때 쓰는 구어체 표현. 공식적인 자리에서는 피해야 하며 부정적인 뉘앙스가 강함.
expressions.example: He knocked her up and then disappeared. → 그는 그녀를 임신시키고 사라져버렸어.

occ.speaker: The Woman From Poughkeepsie
occ.text: (outside Ross's window) Ross? Ross! (she knocks on the window) Wake up! Ross! (the train starts moving) Ross! Ross!! Ross!!! Ross!!!!

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Nope, not knocked up yet.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Uh-hmm. I'm just thinking about Phoebe; poor knocked up Phoebe.

expressions.expression: If you hit [place], you've gone too far
expressions.dialogue:
Grandmother: He lives at 74 Laurel Drive in Middletown. **If you hit the Dairy Queen, you've gone too far.** You can take my cab.
Phoebe: Wow. Thank you.
expressions.speaker: Grandmother
expressions.nuance: 길 안내에서 'hit'은 특정 지점에 도달하는 것을 뜻함. 일상 대화와 길 안내에서 자주 등장하는 비공식 표현.
expressions.example: If you hit the bridge, you've gone too far — the turn is before it. → 다리에 닿으면 지나친 거야, 그 전에 꺾어야 해.

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: Phoebe here with the cab yet?
Chandler: Yeah, she, she brought the invisible cab. . . **hop in**.
Joey: Well she better get here soon, the outlet stores close at 7.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.nuance: 차나 버스 등에 빠르게 탑승하라는 캐주얼한 초대 표현. 여기서 Chandler는 빈정대며 쓰고 있지만, 실제 상황에서도 자연스럽게 쓰임.
expressions.example: We're already running late — hop in and I'll explain on the way. → 이미 늦었으니까 얼른 타, 가면서 설명할게.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Well, if she's my friend, hopefully she'll understand. I mean, wouldn't you guys?

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Well, you better hop outta the shower, cause... I gotta flush. (lays down cards)

occ.speaker: Rtst
occ.text: No, ma'am. Well, anyhoo, we should be getting our F.D.A. approval any day now, hopefully, in time for Thanksgiving. See, the way we look at it, chocolate already dominates most of your major food-preparation holidays: Easter, Christmas, what have you.

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: Good luck Phoebs.
Phoebe: OK, **here I go**. . . here I go. . . I'm goin'. [she just sits in the cab]
Chandler: Alright.
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: 자, 간다 / 이제 해볼게 (결심하고 시작하려 할 때)
expressions.nuance: 두렵거나 큰 결심이 필요한 일을 막 시작하려 할 때 스스로를 다독이며 하는 말. 실제로 움직이지 못하고 되풀이하는 장면이 유머 포인트가 됨.
expressions.example: OK, here I go. I'm calling him right now. → 자, 간다. 지금 바로 전화할 거야.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: I think we are getting a little ahead of selves here. Okay. Okay. I am just going to get up, go to work and not think about him all day. Or else I'm just gonna get up and go to work.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Janice. Hi, Janice. Ok, here we go. I don't think we should go out anymore. Janice.

occ.speaker: Mrs. Geller
occ.text: Here, sweetie, here. I took these when I had my golfing accident. (Hands Ross a bottle of pills. Then turns to Monica and pats her hair over her ears)

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: OK, tip the man.
Monica: No, if he doesn't like our cookies, too bad, I am not gonna **cave**.
Rachel: Yeah, I'm with Mon.
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.nuance: 협상이나 갈등에서 상대의 요구나 압박에 결국 져서 원하는 걸 들어줄 때 씀. 'I won't cave' 형태로 굳은 의지를 표현하는 데도 자주 쓰임.
expressions.example: I told myself I wouldn't eat sweets, but I caved after seeing the cake. → 단 것 안 먹겠다고 했는데, 케이크 보고 결국 참지 못했어.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Marsha, these are cave people. Okay? They have issues like 'Gee, that glacier's getting kinda close.' See?

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: I remember the day I got my first pay check. There was a cave in in one of the mines, and eight people were killed.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Hey, you're not him. You're you. When they were all over you to go into your father's pipe-fitting business, did you cave?

expressions.dialogue:
Rachel: you don't just sort of **seize the day**, you know. You like me for what, a year, you didn't do anything about it.
[Later] Ross: you know how you say I never **seize the day**? Well, alright, even though he's your super, **I'm seizing**. [approaches Mr. Treeger]
Rachel: Nice seizing. . . gel boy.
expressions.speaker: Rachel / Ross
expressions.nuance: 라틴어 carpe diem에서 온 표현으로, 지금 당장 용기 있게 행동하라는 뜻. Ross가 'I'm seizing'처럼 동사 단독으로 쓴 게 재미있는 포인트.
expressions.example: I was nervous about asking for a raise, but I decided to seize the moment. → 연봉 인상 요청이 두려웠지만, 용기 내서 기회를 잡기로 했어.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: You weren't there! Okay, maybe this is something that I-I'm supposed to seize! Y'know?

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: Looks like he's playin' baseball.
Ross: You mean **hardball**?
Monica: Whatever.
expressions.speaker: Ross (correcting Monica)
expressions.nuance: 양보 없이 강하게 대응하는 협상 전략을 뜻함. Monica가 'baseball'이라고 잘못 말한 것을 Ross가 고쳐주는 장면이 웃음 포인트.
expressions.example: When the landlord wouldn't lower the rent, we decided to play hardball. → 집주인이 월세를 안 낮춰주자 우리는 강경하게 나가기로 했어.

expressions.dialogue:
Phoebe: I'm sorry about your shopping.
Chandler: Oh, that's OK, we'll **figure something out**.
Joey: Uh, listen Phoebs, I know you're not goin' in there but do you think it'd be alright if I went in and used his bathroom?
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.nuance: 문제가 생겼을 때 낙관적으로 '어떻게든 되겠지'라는 뉘앙스로 쓰는 표현. 구체적인 해결책이 없어도 자신감 있게 쓸 수 있음.
expressions.example: I don't know how we'll afford it, but we'll figure something out. → 어떻게 감당할지는 모르겠지만, 어떻게든 방법이 생길 거야.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Look, I don't even know how I feel about him yet. Just give me a chance to figure that out.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: But see, it was a plan. Y'know, it was clear. It was figured out, and now everything's just kinda like...

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: No. I just need to be by myself for a while, you know? I just got to figure out what I want

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: Phoebs, that's OK. You **took a big step** today.
Phoebe: Yeah?
Chandler: Yeah, and someday when you're ready, you'll make it past the hedges.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.nuance: 두렵거나 어려운 일을 시도한 것을 격려할 때 씀. 물리적 행동보다 감정적·심리적 진전을 묘사할 때 주로 사용됨.
expressions.example: Admitting you need help is taking a big step. → 도움이 필요하다고 인정하는 것도 큰 한 발을 내딛는 거야.

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: Oh, hey, do you think there's a chance you could fix that radiator now?
Mr. Treeger: **No can do**, like I told the girl, I can't get a new knob until Thursday.
Monica: So, wait, you really did like my cookies?
expressions.speaker: Mr. Treeger
expressions.nuance: 'I can't do that'을 짧고 단호하게 거절하는 표현. 사과 없이 딱 잘라 말하는 느낌이며, 캐주얼한 상황에서 가볍게 쓸 수 있음.
expressions.example: 'Can you cover my shift tomorrow?' 'No can do, I've got plans.' → '내일 내 시간 대신 일해줄 수 있어?' '그건 안 돼, 약속이 있거든.'

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: My way?! You-you think this is my way? Believe me, of all the ways I ever imagined this moment in my life being, this is not my way- y'know what? Uh, um, this is too hard. I'm not, I can't do-

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: ...I'll go. Let's start with the way he kept picking at- no, I'm sorry, I can't do this, can't do this. We loved him.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Not that big a deal? It's amazing. Ok, you just reach in there, there's one little maneuver, and bam, a bra right out the sleeve. All right, as far as I'm concerned, there is nothing a guy can do that even comes close. Am I right?

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: Phoebs, what's goin' on?
Phoebe: No, it's just like, ya know, it's **a whole mess of** stuff, ya know.
Joey: Well, maybe he's, maybe he's this really cool pharmacist guy.
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: 아주 많은, 복잡하게 얽혀 있는 것들
expressions.nuance: 'a lot of'보다 더 혼란스럽고 감당하기 어려운 양이나 상황을 묘사할 때 씀. 구어체이며 감정적인 무게감이 있음.
expressions.example: There's a whole mess of paperwork to get through before the deadline. → 마감 전에 처리해야 할 서류가 엄청 쌓여 있어.

expressions.dialogue:
Chandler: Phoebs, let me ask you something, were, were these, uh, funny brownies?
Phoebe: **Not especially**. But you know what, I think they had pot in them.
Ross: So you guys, who else did you tip with cookies?
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.nuance: 'No'보다 훨씬 부드럽고 애매한 부정. 확실히 아니라고 하기 애매하거나, 가볍게 부정할 때 쓰는 표현.
expressions.example: 'Was the movie funny?' 'Not especially, but the acting was good.' → '영화 재미있었어?' '딱히 그렇진 않았는데, 연기는 좋더라.'

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Maybe your resolution is to not make fun of your friends, especially the ones who may soon be flying you to Europe for free on their own plane.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: What do you think Monica mean when she said she didn't want to talk, especially with me? I mean, why not especially you and me? We were both out there kissing.

occ.speaker: Tag
occ.text: But I'm not gay. And I especially wouldn't want you to think I was gay.

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: Well this, this is too much, I feel like I should get you another sweater.
Chandler: **And last but not least**.
Joey: They're ribbed for your pleasure.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.nuance: 발표·선물 등에서 마지막 순서지만 앞의 것들과 동등하게(혹은 더) 중요하다는 걸 강조할 때 쓰는 고정 표현. 여기서는 황당한 선물을 소개하는 데 쓰여 웃음 포인트.
expressions.example: And last but not least, I'd like to thank my parents for their support. → 그리고 마지막으로, 하지만 결코 덜 중요하지 않게, 부모님께 감사를 전하고 싶어요.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: And last but not least.

expressions.dialogue:
Phoebe: Um, gram, can I see the pictures of my dad again?
Grandmother: Oh. Oh, sure, sure, uh, **how come**?
Phoebe: Just, you know, to see...
expressions.speaker: Grandmother
expressions.nuance: 'Why'보다 더 캐주얼하고 구어적인 표현. 문법적으로도 차이가 있는데, 'Why are you late?'와 달리 'How come you're late?'처럼 어순이 바뀌지 않음.
expressions.example: 'I'm not going to the party.' 'How come?' → '나 파티에 안 갈 거야.' '왜?'

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Ohh-ho-hooohhh. The hair comes out, and the gloves come on.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: (in baby-like voice) How come you don't live with Mommy? (pause; shows Ross less than amused) How come Mommy lives with that other lady? (pause; Ross still looks less than amused; Joey smiling) What's a lesbian? (playfully hits Ross)

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: No, no... how come you are working here?

expressions.dialogue:
Grandmother: Well, I think you're **better off** without him. Oh honey, I know he's your daddy but to me he's still the irresponsible creep who knocked up your mom.
Phoebe: No I just, just wanted to know who he was, ya know.
expressions.speaker: Grandmother
expressions.meaning: ~없는 게 더 낫다, ~없이 사는 게 더 좋다
expressions.nuance: 누군가나 무언가 없이 지내는 게 더 행복하거나 나은 상황임을 표현할 때 씀. 위로나 조언의 맥락에서 자주 나옴.
expressions.example: Honestly, you're better off without that job — the company was a mess anyway. → 솔직히, 그 직장 없는 게 더 나아, 어차피 회사가 엉망이었잖아.

occ.speaker: Mr. Tribbiani
occ.text: Just for a coupla days. I got a job midtown. I figure I'm better off staying with the kid than hauling my ass back and forth on the ferry. (Sees Roger) I don't know this one.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: No no, uh, don't thank me for comin' in. Uh, at least let me finish. Uh, we could take the expressway but uh, this time of day you're better off taking the budge. You were goin' for the word bridge there weren't ya. I'll have a good day. [gets up and leaves]

occ.speaker: Ryan
occ.text: I better get out of here, I'm gonna miss my flight.

expressions.dialogue:
Grandmother: Now, remember, nobody else drives that cab.
Phoebe: Uh-huh, got it. Ooh, I'm gonna see my dad. **Wish me luck**, Grandpa! [blows a kiss to a picture of Einstein]
[Next scene: Chandler is standing on a street corner]
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.nuance: 두렵거나 중요한 일을 앞두고 주변 사람에게 응원을 요청하는 표현. 대화 상대가 실제로 행운을 빌어줄 수 없는 상황(사진 속 인물)에 쓰면 유머 포인트가 됨.
expressions.example: I have a big interview tomorrow. Wish me luck! → 내일 중요한 면접이 있어. 행운을 빌어줘!

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Oh, look, wish me luck!

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: (gets up) (softly) Wish me luck.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Hi! Wish me luck!
